meta.title: Server/Client Boundaries: What Can and Cannot Cross
meta.excerpt: The most common architectural mistake in Next.js apps: exporting non-async values from 'use server' files. Learn the rules, the TypeScript compiler behaviour, and the module boundary patterns that prevent systemic violations.

props.content: The server/client boundary is the most consequential architectural decision in Next.js. Every component is either a Server Component or a Client Component, and the rules for what can cross between them are strict, often invisible, and the source of the most confusing errors in modern React.

In Next.js App Router, all components are Server Components by default. They execute on the server, have access to the file system, databases, and secrets, and send only their rendered HTML to the browser. Client Components are explicitly opted in with the 'use client' directive and run in the browser with access to state, effects, and browser APIs.

The boundary between them is not just a conceptual divide -- it is a serialisation barrier. Data crossing from server to client must be serialisable to JSON. This single constraint drives most of the architectural patterns and error messages in Next.js applications.

Only JSON-serialisable values can be passed as props from a Server Component to a Client Component. This is because the server renders the component tree, serialises it into a payload (the RSC payload), and streams it to the browser where React hydrates the Client Components.

props.title: Serialisable Types (Can Cross)
rows: Keys must be strings, values serialisable
Server Components can pass JSX as children

props.content: JSX elements (React nodes) can cross the boundary as children or props. This is the foundation of the composition pattern -- a Client Component can receive Server Component children without those children becoming Client Components.

props.title: Non-Serialisable Types (Cannot Cross)
rows: Functions cannot be passed directly to Client Components
Only plain objects can be passed
Browser-only, does not exist on server
Functions -- must be defined in client
Hooks only work in Client Components

props.content: Think of the boundary as a JSON.stringify/JSON.parse gate. If you cannot run JSON.stringify on the value and get it back with JSON.parse, it cannot cross. This mental model resolves 90% of boundary errors.

Date objects are a common trap. They serialise to strings, so the client receives a string not a Date. Always convert dates to ISO strings on the server and parse them on the client with new Date(isoString).

The most powerful pattern for working with the boundary is composition: passing Server Components as children to Client Components. The Client Component does not need to know its children are Server Components -- it just renders them.

features.title: Mistake: Making Layout a Client Component
features.description: Adding 'use client' to a layout because it needs one interactive element. This forces ALL children to be Client Components, losing server rendering for the entire subtree.
items: Fix: Extract the interactive part into a small Client Component
Keep the layout as a Server Component
Pass the Client Component as a child

features.title: Mistake: Importing Server-Only Code in Client
features.description: Importing a database utility or API key into a Client Component. The bundler will try to include it in the client bundle, leaking secrets or crashing.
items: Fix: Use the 'server-only' package to mark modules
Server-only imports throw at build time if used in client
Always separate server utilities from shared utilities

features.title: Mistake: useState for Data That Does Not Change
features.description: Using useState to hold data fetched from an API, when the data is available at render time. This unnecessarily makes the component a Client Component.
items: Fix: Fetch in a Server Component, pass as props
Only use useState for truly dynamic client state
Think: does this data change after first render?

features.title: Mistake: Wrapping Everything in Providers
features.description: Wrapping the entire app in Context providers in the root layout. React Context requires 'use client', so this makes the root layout a Client Component.
items: Fix: Create a separate Providers Client Component
Import it in the Server Component layout

decisions.condition: Uses useState, useEffect, or event handlers?
Fetches data or accesses secrets/env variables?

decisions.result: Evaluate case-by-case, default to Server Component

props.title: Server vs Client Component Capabilities
rows: Via API routes or Server Actions

props.content: Understanding the boundary changes how you architect entire applications. The goal is to push as much as possible to Server Components (zero bundle cost, direct data access) and use Client Components only for the interactive leaf nodes.

props.title: Architecture Principle: Push Down the Client Boundary

steps.title: Start with Everything as Server Components
steps.description: The default is server. Only add 'use client' when you encounter a specific need (hooks, events, browser APIs). This gives you the smallest possible client bundle.

steps.title: Identify Interactive Leaf Nodes
steps.description: Find the specific elements that need interactivity: buttons, form inputs, dropdowns, modals. These are your Client Components -- keep them small and focused.

steps.title: Extract Client Components Down
steps.description: Instead of making a whole page a Client Component, extract just the interactive part. A 500-line page with one dropdown should have a 20-line Client Component for the dropdown.

steps.title: Use Composition to Connect Them
steps.description: Server Components fetch data and render structure. Client Components handle interaction. Use children/props composition to wire them together without leaking the boundary upward.

props.content: The server/client boundary is a serialisation barrier, not a suggestion. Design your component tree so that Server Components handle data and structure at the top, and Client Components handle interactivity at the leaves. Use composition (children, JSX props) to connect them. When in doubt, ask: 'Can I JSON.stringify this prop?' If not, it cannot cross. Master this one concept and most Next.js App Router confusion disappears.
